abstract:
The chemical diversity of natural antioxidants (AOXs) presents significant challenges for the separation, detection, and quantification of individual compounds within complex food and biological matrices. Because individual antioxidant species often work cooperatively, measuring total antioxidant power is frequently more biologically relevant for evaluating health benefits than quantifying single components. However, there is currently no universal antioxidant assay suitable for food labeling, largely due to a lack of standardized quantification protocols.

Existing antioxidant assays are generally categorized into two groups based on their underlying mechanisms: electron transfer (ET) and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT). Because these assays differ in their redox potentials, pH requirements, solvent dependencies, and reaction mechanisms, the results they produce are often difficult to compare directly. This project aims to address these discrepancies by providing a more rational framework for classifying antioxidant assays, identifying their specific constraints and challenges, and elucidating the mutual effects of various antioxidants. By integrating the collaborative expertise and practical experience of the task group members across diverse measurement protocols, this work seeks to make antioxidant research more comparable, understandable, and scientifically robust.
